title: Random Quest: Dungeon Delve - straddling the line between boxed card/board game and solo RPG
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nyHN_s3QpkY

Daniel (@dungeondive) examines Random Quest: Dungeon Delve, a Kickstarted solo dungeon-crawl by Brian Larch (art by Carol Chengoff): roll up a character, clear randomly-built dungeons of monsters/traps, bank loot and XP, and climb from basic to advanced dungeons to win the title of Majestic Lord/Lady of Renown. It's a d6, roll-under system — five primaries (combat, agility, health, knowledge, personality) each split into two sub-abilities — with a card-based dungeon, a generous black-and-white bestiary (the art's highlight), and monster reaction rolls that keep combat unpredictable.

His core observation: the game has an identity crisis, straddling boxed board/card game and solo RPG. It openly tells you to adjudicate situations with no clear rule, which players from a strict-board-game background may find jarring (RPG folks won't). He flags genuine ambiguities — how group combat resolves, dual-wielding, carry limits — and warns combat against large groups can drag.

He's warm on the Kickstarter spirit (one creator's passion project, shipped early) and loves the random-generator cards, but in a crowded niche points to Four Against Darkness, 2D6 Dungeon, or pairing Axebane's Deck of Many Dungeons with a light system (Rogueland, Harper's Quest) as alternatives. Can't decide it for you, but lays out the trade-offs.

----

Does a game straddling board game and solo RPG appeal to you, or do you want it to firmly pick a lane?
